perception.who_is_this_for_a: A Disney trip planner who wants a simple cancellation alert, with later sections for planners, superfans, agents, safety, comparisons, plans, testimonials, and agency inquiry.
perception.who_is_this_for_b: A Disney trip planner trying to recover specific hard-to-get reservations, with strong coverage proof and clearer expert-facing Pro comparison later on.

reasoning: Candidate B better satisfies the core selling job from the rendered page. Its first viewport has a complete free-alert form with restaurant, trip dates, party size, email, and a strong CTA, so a visitor can immediately understand and begin the primary action. It also states no Disney login is required near the hero, explains the official Disney handoff, and uses the headline to name high-intent restaurants. The full page supports coverage confidence with many restaurant cards, clear workflow, metrics, pricing, comparison details, FAQ, and agency path without letting the agency path dominate. Candidate A has broader segment content and a useful sample alert, but the desktop first viewport feels less resolved: the form appears duplicated, the right side proof is sparse, and mobile hides the actual criteria inputs behind a CTA, making the first task less direct. A is credible and comprehensive, but B is more conversion-ready, more specific to search intent, and better aligned with the diagnosis emphasis on immediate task clarity plus early trust.
blocking_visual_issue_a: Mobile first viewport shows a CTA but not the actual alert setup fields, and desktop appears to duplicate the Start Free Alert button/form area, which weakens task confidence.
